title: Star Wars: Yoda Stories

'''''Star Wars: Yoda Stories''''' is a [[1997]] [[Star Wars Legends|''Star Wars'' Legends]] [[Star Wars video games|video game]] developed by [[LucasArts]] for [[Wikipedia:Personal computer|PC]] and [[Wikipedia:Game Boy Color|Game Boy Color]]. It was preceded by [[Wikipedia:Indiana Jones and His Desktop Adventures|''Indiana Jones and His Desktop Adventures'']], and no further ''Desktop Adventures'' titles were released. In the ''Star Wars'' timeline, ''Yoda Stories'' is placed between [[Star Wars: Episode V The Empire Strikes Back|''Star Wars'': Episode V ''The Empire Strikes Back'']] and [[Star Wars: Episode VI Return of the Jedi|''Star Wars'': Episode VI ''Return of the Jedi'']], where [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]] is completing his training with Jedi Master [[Yoda/Legends|Yoda]].

==Plot summary==
{{Quote|Well done, Luke! Taken another step you have... along the path that never ends!|Yoda, to [[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke Skywalker]]|<ref name="Star Wars: Yoda Stories">''[[Star Wars: Yoda Stories]]''</ref>}}
Unlike other titles, ''Yoda Stories'' has no central plot or definitive goal—it's a collection of random games where the player, controlling Luke Skywalker, has to complete an objective like rescuing [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]] or [[Princess/Legends|Princess]] [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Leia]], obtaining an object, or warning the Rebellion in several locations of the ''Star Wars'' universe like [[Neshtab]], [[Tatooine/Legends|Tatooine]], and the [[Endor/Legends|Forest moon of Endor]].

==Gameplay==
[[File:Yoda_stories.png|thumb|left|250px|[[Luke Skywalker/Legends|Luke]] rescuing [[Yoda/Legends|Yoda]] from [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]] and two [[Cold assault stormtrooper|snowtroopers]]]]
Each game starts with Luke arriving at [[Dagobah/Legends|Dagobah]] in an [[T-65 X-wing starfighter/Legends|X-wing]]. If the player is still a rookie, [[R2-D2/Legends|R2-D2]] can be picked up and dropped anytime for hints. After finding Yoda, the Jedi Master gives Luke one assignment and one object that will be required to complete it, and the game begins. Each gameworld is composed by 100 screens, but how many are active can be set in the options. Although it's not required to complete the game, a [[Spot-on locator|locator]] provides information (such as the status of a puzzle) and a way to find out where the player is. It is always found in one of the eight screens surrounding the spaceport screen, usually found under a rock, inside a wrecked vehicle or with a person. To progress in the game, the player has to make errands, such as finding a key, or an object, until he reaches the final puzzle. [[The Force/Legends|The Force]] is also present, but in the beginning it must be obtained from the ghost of [[Obi-Wan Kenobi/Legends|Obi-Wan Kenobi]]. It stops all enemies from moving, and is occasionally required to complete a puzzle.

There are several enemies, from [[Galactic Empire/Legends|Imperial]] [[Stormtrooper/Legends|stormtroopers]] and [[Tusken Raider/Legends|Tusken Raiders]] to animals, and all can be dealt accordingly with Luke's [[Lightsaber/Legends|lightsaber]], blasters or thermal detonators (that destroy any enemies in a 3-radius plus-sign pattern). As Luke can be hurt, there are several ways to replenish his health, from health kits and [[Bacta/Legends|bacta]] canisters to plants.

In the fifth game, Luke battles [[Anakin Skywalker/Legends|Darth Vader]], and if successful, the next game he starts with a green lightsaber instead of his father's blue; after 10, he starts with the Force (without the need to find the ghost of Obi-Wan), and finally, after 15 times, with a "Spirit Heart," which gives the player a second life. Unlike ''Indiana Jones and his Desktop Adventures'', ''Yoda Stories'' keeps the number of games beaten and also the highest score.

[[Indiana Jones]] is featured as an [[Easter egg (virtual)|easter egg]] in a mission, as a sequel to a mission in ''Desktop Adventures''. Luke remarks on his similarity to Han Solo.

===Possible scenarios===
The order of missions, the planets' maps and other details are randomly chosen, but the goals and which planet a mission takes place on stays the same each time.

'''Desert World'''
*'''[[Bakkah]]''': [[Mission to Bakkah|Recover]] [[Sith/Legends|Sith]] Amulet
*'''[[Tozeer]]''': Find and rescue [[C-3PO/Legends|C-3PO]]
*'''[[Halm]]''': Retrieve an [[Adegan crystal/Legends|Adegan crystal]]
*'''Tatooine''': Rescue [[Han Solo/Legends|Han Solo]] from [[Jabba Desilijic Tiure/Legends|Jabba the Hutt]]
*'''Tatooine''': Release the impounded [[Millennium Falcon/Legends|''Millennium Falcon'']]

'''Forest World'''
*'''[[Nibiru]]''': [[Mission to Nibiru|Find and rescue]] [[Leia Organa Solo/Legends|Princess Leia]]
*'''[[Endor/Legends|Endor]]''': Retrieve the [[Ewok/Legends|Ewoks]]' [[Lantern of Sacred Light]]
*'''[[Varn]]''': Sabotage Imperial [[Battlemoon]]
*'''[[Tarsa]]''': Sabotage Imperial relay station
*'''[[Argavat]]''': Recover your [[T-65 X-wing starfighter/Legends|X-wing]] from forest world, travel to Agravat to retrieve Imperial Battle Code

'''Snow World'''
*'''[[Etorasp]]''': Rescue [[General/Legends|General]] [[Marutz]]
*'''[[Thaldo]]''': Stop an [[Battle of Thaldo|Imperial attack]] against a Rebel base
*'''[[Neshtab]]''': Sabotage a secret Imperial factory (which seems to be producing [[Dark Trooper/Legends|dark troopers]])
*'''[[Lokondo]]''': Find and rescue [[Yoda/Legends|Yoda]]
*'''[[Unidentified planet (cloning machine)|Unidentified planet]]''': Find and destroy a cloning machine (Indiana Jones cameo appearance in this scenario)

==Development==
[[File:YodaStories_GBC.jpg|thumb|150px|Box art from Gameboy Color version]]
A [[Wikipedia:Microsoft Windows|Windows]] version was released in [[1997]]. This version can be bought on disc now, but needs a download from the internet to get it to work on newer versions of Windows. The Windows version included a re-release of ''[[Star Wars: Making Magic]]''.

The [[Wikipedia:Game Boy Color|Game Boy Color]] version was released in [[1999]]. Like the computer version, the gameplay is a number of simple quests, but the game consisted of a finite number of levels: fifteen. The graphics are not as detailed or colorful compared to the computer game due to the limitations of the Game Boy Color hardware, and controlling the main character is more difficult and less fluid in this version due to the lack of a mouse. Because of these factors and lackluster, repetitive gameplay, the game has received consistently low scores from several gaming sites; Gamespot cited it as "possibly the worst Star Wars game ever produced."

==Continuity==
The game was "made purely for fun" rather than to fit within the continuity of the [[Star Wars Legends|Expanded Universe]]<ref name="Holocron continuity">{{SWBoards|url=thread.jspa?threadID=152583&start=02178#13682994|thread=Holocron continuity database questions|author=[[Leland Chee|Leland Y Chee]]|date=2005-12-12|time=12:17 PM|quote=The short answer is, the stuff that fits. There has definitely been a greater effort to make newer games fit within the rest EU than in years past. Then again, some games are made purely for fun such as games like Demolition, Master of Teras Kasi, Yoda Stories, and Super Bombad Racing.}}</ref> according to [[Leland Chee]], who maintains the [[Holocron continuity database]].<ref name="LinkedIn">{{LinkedIn|url=in/holocron|text=Leland Chee|archiveurl=https://archive.is/byVHT}}</ref>
